title: Creating a Custom Print Dialog
text: Creating a Custom Print Dialog You can create your own print dialog box that includes an operation to handle printing. This operation will incorporate the print statement, which requires the following information: print { PrintJobModel {, PrintMode {, Settings }}} Copy To create a custom print dialog box: Create a Print dialog box as a modal form. Define an operation similar to the following: operation MYPRINT params string $PRINTSETTINGS$ : INOUT endparams ... Copy $PRINTSETTINGS$ is a component variable containing an associative list. Default print settings can be passed to the Print dialog by $PRINTSETTINGS$ , and the modified print settings can be returned to the form by the same parameter. The dialog box only has to return information about PrintJobModel , PrintMode and Settings This is because /ask is redundant in a custom print dialog box, and print /preview should be activated directly from the parent form and not from the Print dialog, as the data required to build the print preview is not available in the Print dialog. Enable the form to specify print settings, using print job models or third-party software. When making exclusive use of print job models, do the following: Create a drop-down list of available print job models, using a ValRep in the format of PrintJobModel = DescriptiveText . Create a drop-down list to select PrintMode . Create a field to set the value for Settings . Return the user’s choices to the parent form by $PRINTSETTINGS$ . Process the list to provide PrintJobModel , PrintMode , and Settings information to the print statement. To extend the custom print dialog box to use a third-party DLL to change Microsoft Windows printer settings. For more information, see Use Third-Party DLLs in a Custom Print Dialog . Related Links print Activate printing. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
